Page | 5 

 1. Introduction

This document provides basic information on using the Nimbus-E Microwave Spectrometer (NEMS) 
Level 2 Output Data product.

1.1 Data Product Description

The Nimbus-E Microwave Spectrometer (NEMS) Level-2 Output Data product contains surface 
reflectivity, water vapor, liquid water, thickness, temperature at standard pressure levels, surface 
brightness temperature, and surface type information, as well as the input antenna and brightness 
temperatures at 5 microwave channels. The data were originally created on IBM 360/165 computers 
and copied to 1600 bpi 9-track magnetic tapes. The data are grouped into 16-second, earth-viewing 
frames. National Meteorological Center (NMC) grid data, interpolated in time and space to the 
NEMS measurement point, are sometimes included.

The data are available for the time period from 17 December 1972 to 31 October 1973. Each data 
file contains about 3-7 days worth of data. The principal investigators for the NEMS experiment was 
Prof. David H. Staelin from MIT.

This product was previously available from the NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDC) 
under the name Microwave Spectrometer Output Files (NEMSOT) with the identifier ESAD-00129 
(old id 72-097A-03A).

1.1.1 The Nimbus-E Microwave Spectrometer

The Nimbus E Microwave Spectrometer (NEMS) was designed primarily to demonstrate the 
capabilities and limitations of microwave sensors for measuring tropospheric temperature profiles, 
water vapor abundances, cloud liquid water content, and earth surface temperatures. The NEMS 
continuously monitored microwave radiation at frequencies of 22.235 and 31.4 GHz (H2O channels), 
and 53.65, 54.9 and 58.8 GHz (O2 channels) in the nadir. The three channels near the 5-mm oxygen 
(O2) absorption band were used primarily to determine the atmospheric temperature profiles. NEMS
provided measurements even in cloud cover conditions that normally restrict the usefulness of 
conventional IR data in such situations. The two water vapor (H2O) channels near 10 mm permitted 
the water vapor and cloud liquid water content over oceans to be estimated and also to yield an 
estimated temperature once the surface emissivity had been calibrated by comparison with direct 
measurements. The NEMS viewed footprint is a 180-km diameter circle on the earth's surface.

Data are available for the time period from 1972-12-17 to 1973-10-31. An advanced version of this 
instrument, the Scanning Microwave Spectrometer (SCAMS), was flown on the subsequent Nimbus 6
satellite.

Page | 6 

 1.1.2 Nimbus-5 Overview

The Nimbus 5 satellite was successfully launched on December 11, 1972. The primary experiments
included:   (1)   a   Temperature-Humidity   Infrared   Radiometer   (THIR)   for   measuring   day   and   night
surface and cloud top temperatures, as well as the water vapor content of the upper atmosphere,
(2) an Electrically Scanning Microwave Radiometer (ESMR) for mapping the microwave radiation
from the Earth's surface and atmosphere, (3) an Nimbus-E Microwave Spectrometer (NEMS) for
obtaining vertical profiles of temperature and moisture, (4) the Nimbus-E Microwave Spectrometer
(NEMS) for determining tropospheric temperature profiles, atmospheric water vapor abundances,
and cloud liquid water contents, (5) a Selective Chopper Radiometer (SCR) for observing the global
temperature   structure   of   the   atmosphere,   and   (6)   a   Surface   Composition   Mapping   Radiometer
(SCMR) for measuring the differences in the thermal emission characteristics of the Earth's surface.

The orbit of the satellite can be characterized by the following:

 circular orbit at 1100 km

 inclination of 99.9 degrees

 period of an orbit is about 107.2 minutes

 orbits cross the equator at 26 degrees of longitude separation

 sun-synchronous

1.2 Algorithm Background

The Nimbus-5 NEMS data were generated from the spacecraft telemetry, attitude and orbital data .
The data were originally processed on IBM 370/165 computers, and copied to 1600 bpi 9-track tapes
for archival.  Further information on the NEMS instrument and data processing can be found in the
Nimbus-5 Users' Guide Section 7.

1.3 Data Disclaimer

The data should be used with care and one should first read the Nimbus-5 User’s Guide, Section 7 
describing the NEMS experiment. Users should cite this data product in their research:

Staelin, David H. (2020), NEMS/Nimbus-5 Level 2 Output Data V001, Greenbelt, MD, USA, Goddard 
Earth Sciences Data and Information Services Center (GES DISC), Accessed: [Data Access Date], 
https://doi.org/10.5067/4SN4932GXC8R

Page | 7 

 2. Data Organization

The Nimbus-E Microwave Spectrometer (NEMS) Level-2 Output Data spans the time period from 
December 17, 1972 to October 31, 1973. A file typically contains about 3-7 days worth of data.

2.1 File Naming Convention

The data product files are named according to the following convention:

<Platform>-<Instrument>_<Level>_<DateTime>_<TapeNumber>.<Suffix>, where

o Platform = name of the platform or satellite (Nimbus5)
o) Instrument = name of the instrument and product (NEMS)
o) Level = process level (L2)
o) Date = Data start date in format <YYYY>m<MMDD> where

1. YYYY = 4 digit year (1972 – 1973)
2. MM = 2 digit month (01 - 12)
3. DD = 2 digit day of month (01 - 31)
4. hh = 2 digit hour of day (00 - 23)
5. mm = 2 digit minute of hour (00 – 59)
ss = 2 digit second of minute (00 - 59)
6.

o TapeNumber = 2 digit number of tape (preceded by ‘DR’ - primary or ‘DS’ - backup)
o Suffix = the file format (always TAP, indicating tape binary data)

File name example: Nimbus5-NEMS_L2_1972m1217t011144_DR24.TAP

2.2 File Format and Structure

The data are stored as they were originally written in IBM binary (big-endian) record oriented 
structured files. The files were written on the original 1600 bpi 9-track tapes using a blocked 
FORTRAN format. Each tape has about 8 files on it, with each file containing about 3-7 days worth of 
data. Each data file on the tape contains a set of data records with a FORTRAN record size word, the 
record block, and a FORTRAN record trailing size word.

Each data record in a file is 11,624 bytes in size. Files end with a single End-of-File word, the last file 
on the tape is followed by a double End-of-File word. Each record block contains the data for a NEMS
calibration cycle, which normally consists of 16 major frames, the first 14 of these are Earth viewing 
data, and the last two contain the cold-load and ambient-load calibration information. The data are 
written in big-endian IBM 16-bit or 32-bit integers, or IBM 32-bit floats. For the contents and layout 
of the data, see section 3.1.

During data recovery there were two sets of thirty-one tapes, each containing 8 or 7 files. The first 
set of tapes are the primary tapes and designated with a DR. The second set are the backup tapes 

Page | 8 

 which are designated with a DS. The DR and DS tape files are for the most part are all identical, 
except four files on the DS backup tapes were each missing one data record compared to the 
counterpart on the primary tape. Therefore the 31 files from the primary DR tapes represent the 
complete record of the NEMS Level 2 data collection and are publicly available at the GES DISC.

2.3 Key Science Data Fields

The primary science data fields are atmospheric temperature, water vapor, liquid water, surface 
reflectivity, layer thickness, and surface brightness temperature.

Figure 1: Data coverage for a NEMS Level 2 data file showing integrated water vapor.

Page | 9 

 Figure 2: NEMS Level 2 atmospheric temperature profile

Page | 10 

 3. Data Contents

The granularity for the NEMS data is the entire mission with daily resolution.

3.1 Data Records

The Nimbus-5 User’s Guide does not describe the NEMS file format. Refer instead to the 9 page 
“Format of Stacked NEMS Scan Data” document for the file format.

The original tapes contain data files that have between 3 and 7 days worth of data, with each day 
containing ~14 orbits. Each 11,624 byte data record contains the data for a NEMS calibration cycle, 
which consists of 16 major frames. Data were written as IBM big-endian 16-bit integers, 32 bit 
integers, or 32-bit floats. There are 31 data files for the entire mission spanning the 10 ½ month 
time period from December 17, 1972 to October 31, 1973

Page | 15 

 4. Reading the Data

The data are written in a binary record-oriented format. Using the record format specification in the 
section above, users can write software to read the data files. Please note that the data were 
originally written using a big-endian format, therefore users on little-endian machines will need to 
swap bytes for the words. In the PARAMS array, unused values are set to ‘FFFFFFFF’x which will 
translate into IEEE float -Inf. Unused LAT/LON Array values will be both set to 0.00000, and unused 
FLAT/FLON values will be both set to -100.000.

A sample FORTRAN program is included in the Appendix section which will read and print the the 
data contents. Additionally two FORTRAN functions are included to perform byte swapping, and 
another will convert to IEEE float.

Page | 16 

 5. Data Services

5.1 GES DISC Search

The GES DISC provides a keyword, spatial, temporal and advanced (event) searches through its 
unified search and download interface:

https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/

5.2 Documentation

The data product landing pages provide information about these data products, as well as links to 
download the data files and relevant documentation:

https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/datacollection/NEMSN5L2_001.html

5.3 Direct Download

These data products are available for users to download directly using HTTPS:

https://acdisc.gesdisc.eosdis.nasa.gov/data/Nimbus5_NEMS_Level2/NEMSN5L2.001/

 FORTRAN Code

C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
C ^NAME: READ_NEMS
C   This program will read both the Nimbus 5 NEMS Level 2 Output Data.
C
C   The files contain data records of size 11624 bytes. This program will
C   print the contents of every data record.
C
C ^MAJOR VARIABLES:
C     FNAME  - name of input file
C     BUFF   - buffer for data record
C     TEMP   - buffer for holding temporary 4-byte word
C     WORD   - integer 4-byte word
C     IBLKSZ - size of record block in bytes
C     IOS    - I/O status number
C
C ^NOTES:
C     Compile: gfortran -o READ_NEMS.EXE READ_NEMS.FOR
C
C ^ORGANIZATION: NASA/GSFC, Code 610.2
C
C ^AUTHOR:  James Johnson
C
C ^ADDRESS: james.johnson@nasa.gov
C
C ^CREATED: Dec. 18, 2020
C-----------------------------------------------------------------------

      CHARACTER          FNAME*256      ! Filename
      CHARACTER          BUFF(11624)    ! Buffer for data record block
      INTEGER*4          IBLKSZ         ! Size of records
      INTEGER*4          IWORD          ! 4-byte word
      CHARACTER          TEMP(4)        ! Buffer to hold 4-byte word
      EQUIVALENCE        (TEMP,IWORD)

C Get the name of the input data file to read
      WRITE (0, *), 'Enter the name of the input file:'
      READ (5,'(A)') FNAME
      PRINT '("FILE = ",A)', FNAME

C Open the specified input file
      OPEN (UNIT=1, FILE=FNAME, STATUS='OLD', ACCESS='DIRECT',
     &      FORM='UNFORMATTED', RECL=1, ERR=99, IOSTAT=IOS)

C Initialize N (record number) and IOFF (byte offset in file)
      N=0
      IOFF=0

C Loop through the file reading all records in file
   5  DO

C Read the first 4-byte word or record size header
   10   DO I=1,4
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS, ERR=90) TEMP(I)
        END DO

Page | 20 

         IBLKSZ = IWORD
        IOFF=IOFF+(I-1)

C End-of-File (EOF) mark, exit loop
        IF (IBLKSZ .EQ. 0) EXIT

C Next read the block of data
   20   DO I=1,IBLKSZ
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS) BUFF(I)
          IF (IOS .NE. 0) THEN
            PRINT '("ERROR: BUFF ",I4,X,I4,", IOSTAT: ",I6)', N,I-1,IOS
            STOP
          END IF
        END DO
        IOFF=IOFF+(I-1)
        N=N+1

        PRINT '("==== REC ",I4," ===")', N
        CALL PRDREC(IBLKSZ,BUFF)

C Finally read the last 4-byte word (should match first record size)
   30   DO I=1,4
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS, ERR=90) TEMP(I)
        END DO
        IF (IBLKSZ .NE. IWORD) THEN
          PRINT '("WARNING: IBLKSZ ",I10," != ",I10)', IBLKSZ, IWORD
        END IF
        IOFF=IOFF+(I-1)

      END DO

C Close the input file
   90 CLOSE(1)
      STOP

   99 PRINT '("ERROR: OPEN FILE, IOSTAT: ",I6)', IOS

  100 STOP
      END

Page | 21 

 C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
C     This Subroutine will print the Data Records
C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
      SUBROUTINE PRDREC(IBLKSZ,BUFF)

      CHARACTER BUFF(11624)                          ! Buffer for record block
      INTEGER*2 ITNEMS(5,8,18),IHNEMS(40,18),        ! Short Int Arrays
     +          HDRMJF(30,16)
      INTEGER*4 NFRC(14)                             ! Long Int Array
      REAL*4    LAT(16),LON(16),TANEMS(5,4,16),      ! Float Arrays
     +          HKNEMS(38,2),TBNEMS(5,14),
     +          PARAMS(25,4,14),FLAT(16),FLON(16)
      INTEGER*2 I2SWAP                               ! Function swaps short ints
      INTEGER*4 I4SWAP                               ! Function swaps long ints
      REAL*4    R4IBM                                ! IBM float to IEEE float

C     Block/Segment Descriptors
      PRINT '("BLKDSC =",2(X,I8))',  I2SWAP(BUFF(1:2)),I2SWAP(BUFF(3:4))
      PRINT '("SEGDSC =",2(X,I8),/)',I2SWAP(BUFF(5:6)),I2SWAP(BUFF(7:8))

C     Antenna Temperature Counts
      N=9
      DO MF=1,18
        DO IM=1,8
          DO IC=1,5
            ITNEMS(IC,IM,MF) = I2SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1))
            N = N+2
          END DO
        END DO
      END DO
      PRINT '("ITNEMS = ",18(/,8(5(X,I6),/)))', ITNEMS

C     Submultiplex Housekeeping/Data
      DO MF=1,18
        DO ISM=1,40
          IHNEMS(ISM,MF) = I2SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1))
          N = N+2
        END DO
      END DO
      PRINT '("IHNEMS = ",/,18(4(10(X,I6),/)))', IHNEMS

C     Header for each Major Frame
      DO MF=1,16
        DO I=1,30
          HDRMJF(I,MF) = I2SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1))
          N = N+2
        END DO
      END DO
      PRINT '("HDRMJF = ",/,16(3(10(X,I6),/)))', HDRMJF

C     Latitude for Start of Major Frame (missing set to 0.00000)
      DO MF=1,16
        LAT(MF) = R4IBM(I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1)))
        N = N+4
      END DO
      PRINT '("LAT    = ",/,2(6(X,G12.6),/),4(X,G12.6),/)', LAT

Page | 22 

       
 C     Longitude for Start of Major Frame (missing set to 0.00000)
      DO MF=1,16
        LON(MF) = R4IBM(I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1)))
        N = N+4
      END DO
      PRINT '("LON    = ",/,2(6(X,G12.6),/),4(X,G12.6),/)', LON

C     Antenna Temperatures
      DO MF=1,16
        DO I=1,4
          DO IC=1,5
            TANEMS(IC,I,MF) = R4IBM(I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1)))
            N = N+4
          END DO
        END DO
      END DO
      PRINT '("TANEMS = ",16(/,4(5(X,G12.6),/)))', TANEMS

C     Housekeeping Calibration Cycle
      DO J=1,2
        DO I=1,38
          HKNEMS(I,J) = R4IBM(I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1)))
          N = N+4
        END DO
      END DO
      PRINT '("HKNEMS = ",/,(6(6(X,G12.6),/),2(X,G12.6),/))', HKNEMS

C     Brightness Temperatures
      DO MF=1,14
        DO IC=1,5
          TBNEMS(IC,MF) = R4IBM(I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1)))
          N = N+4
        END DO
      END DO
      PRINT '("TBNEMS = ",/,14(5(X,G12.6),/))', TBNEMS

C     Parameters (unused set to 'FFFFFFFF'X translates to IEEE -Inf)
      DO MF=1,14
        DO IY=1,4
          DO IX=1,25
            PARAMS(IX,IY,MF) = R4IBM(I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1)))
            N = N+4
          END DO
        END DO
      END DO
      PRINT '("PARAMS = ",14(/,4(4(6(X,G12.6),/),X,G12.6,/)))', PARAMS

C     NMC Flags
      DO MF=1,14
        NFRC(MF) = I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1))
        N = N+4
      END DO
      PRINT '("NFRC   = ",/,11(X,I6),/,3(X,I6),/)', NFRC

Page | 23 

 C     Fixed Latitude for Middle of Major Frame (missing set to -100.0)
      DO MF=1,16
        FLAT(MF) = R4IBM(I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1)))
        N = N+4
      END DO
      PRINT '("FLAT   = ",/,2(6(X,G12.6),/),4(X,G12.6),/)', FLAT

C     Fixed Longitude for Middle of Major Frame (missing set to -100.0)
      DO MF=1,16
        FLON(MF) = R4IBM(I4SWAP(BUFF(N:N+1)))
        N = N+4
      END DO
      PRINT '("FLON   = ",/,2(6(X,G12.6),/),4(X,G12.6),/)', FLON

      RETURN 
      END

C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
C ^FUNCTION: I2SWAP
C
C     This function will swap the bytes of a 2-byte word
C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
      INTEGER*2 FUNCTION I2SWAP(BUFF)

      CHARACTER          BUFF(2)        ! Input data buffer
      CHARACTER          TEMP(2)        ! Output swapped buffer
      INTEGER*2          I2BUFF
      EQUIVALENCE        (TEMP, I2BUFF)

      TEMP(1) = BUFF(2)
      TEMP(2) = BUFF(1)
      I2SWAP = I2BUFF

      RETURN
      END

C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
C ^FUNCTION: I4SWAP
C
C     This function will swap the bytes of a 4-byte word
C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
      INTEGER*4 FUNCTION I4SWAP(BUFF)

      CHARACTER          BUFF(4)        ! Input data buffer
      CHARACTER          TEMP(4)        ! Output swapped buffer
      INTEGER*4          I4BUFF
      EQUIVALENCE        (TEMP, I4BUFF)

      TEMP(1) = BUFF(4)
      TEMP(2) = BUFF(3)
      TEMP(3) = BUFF(2)
      TEMP(4) = BUFF(1)
      I4SWAP = I4BUFF

      RETURN
      END

Page | 24 

 C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
C ^FUNCTION: R4IBM
C
C     This function will convert an input word to an IBM float
C-----------------------------------------------------------------------
      REAL*4 FUNCTION R4IBM(IWORD)

      INTEGER*4      IDROW              ! reverse the bits of input word
      REAL*8         A /16.0/           ! base number
      INTEGER*4      B /64/             ! exponent offset
      REAL*8         C /0.0/            ! fraction offset
      INTEGER*1      S                  ! sign flag
      INTEGER*2      E                  ! binary exponent
      REAL*8         F                  ! binary fraction
      REAL*8         M                  ! mantissa
      REAL*8         V                  ! float value
      INTEGER*4      I                  ! counter

      S = ISHFT(IWORD, -31)

      E = 0
      DO 10 I=0,6
        E = E + IAND(ISHFT(IWORD,-24),ISHFT(1,I))
   10 END DO

      IDROW = 0
      DO 11 I=0,31
        IF (IAND(IWORD,ISHFT(1,I)) .NE. 0) THEN
          IDROW = IOR(IDROW,(ISHFT(1,31-I)))
        END IF
   11 END DO

      F = 0.0
      DO 12 I=0,31
        IF (ISHFT(IAND(ISHFT(IDROW,-8),ISHFT(1,I)),1) .NE. 0) THEN
          F = F + 1 / FLOAT(ISHFT(IAND(ISHFT(IDROW,-8),ISHFT(1,I)),1))
        END IF
   12 END DO

      M = C + F                         ! calculate the mantissa
      V = (-1)**S * M * A**(E - B)      ! calculate the float value
      IF (ABS(V) .LT. 2.0**(-149)) THEN
        V = (-1)**S * 0.0               ! avoid underflow
      END IF

      R4IBM = V
      RETURN
      END
